body:
## Problem

No way to invoke a command with a "raw", literal arg, without dealing with command-specific escaping, bar `|`, quote `|`, etc.

Some things like `fnameescape()` exist to deal with this, but that only works if the command expects a filename. And it depends on escaping rules, which can be fragile.

## Expected behavior

    :nospecial cmd arg

invokes `:cmd` with literal raw `arg`, including all whitespace.

    :2nospecial cmd arg1 arg2

invokes `:cmd` with literal `arg1` and `arg2`, split at the first whitespace. Does not support "escaping" whitespace, thus `:2nospecial cmd arg1\ arg2` passes `arg1\` literally as arg1.

## use cases

- when doing `:edit`, `:!`, etc., I don't want vim's clever `%` stuff, I want it to treat the arg literally:
  ```
  :nospecial edit https://example.com/#foo=bar%baz
  :nospecial !echo "foo#bar%"
  ```
- internal calls to `:help <token>` https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/cadddbff6959a23337d417b000177326abc5a862/src/nvim/normal.c#L3325
- session restore https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/cadddbff6959a23337d417b000177326abc5a862/src/nvim/ex_session.c#L288
- 'isfname' nonsense interfering with `:buffer [foo]`: https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/31494

comment_data.body:
i'm interested  to work on this issue, as far i understand 
>No way to invoke a command with a "raw", literal arg, without dealing with command-specific escaping.

we can't use `fnameescape()` because it will break in many cases ie: `:edit a | b.txt` 
you suggested `:nospecial cmd arg` with prefix as no of args 2,3.. 

`nv_colon()`  responsible for handling `:` and it call `do_cmdline()` to get and execute cmd.
https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/cadddbff6959a23337d417b000177326abc5a862/src/nvim/normal.c#L3167-L3168

my initial thoughts is to register cmd `nospecial` in `ex_cmds.lua` and implement in `ex_docmd.c`
https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/cadddbff6959a23337d417b000177326abc5a862/src/nvim/ex_cmds.lua#L41-L42

still i'm researching i'll revise this comment if i made some progress. till now am i at right direction?

got it, basically 2 way to implemet this first using modifier cmd like `:noswapfile`, `:keepjumps` etc, other way to acutal cmd like: `:execute`, `:normal`, we can't go with modifier cmd bcz parsing already happing at `do_one_cmd()`. so decided to implement with actual cmd, i reviewed sketch code looks good but not fix the issues ie: symbol parsing already happened,

execution flow:  `do_cmdline_cmd` -> `do_cmdline` -> `do_one_cmd` -> `find_ex_command` -> `separate_nextcmd`(we have to skip this function by tracing `nospecial` to skip parsing) -> `ex_nospecial` -> finally cmd executed.

```diff
+  // Execute the command
+  do_cmdline_cmd(final_cmd.ga_data);
```

i'm thinking to set flag before parsing and skip some parts, anything else i need to keep in mind?

> i reviewed sketch code looks good but not fix the issues ie: symbol parsing already happened,
> ... i'm thinking to set flag before parsing and skip some parts

yes, good catch, exactly. 

btw, for now, let's skip the "count" idea (`:2nospecial cmd arg1 arg2`) if it adds a lot of code. it can always be added later.
paging @neovim/core , am I missing anything that exists already or does this seem useful?

> paging [@neovim/core](https://github.com/orgs/neovim/teams/core) , am I missing anything that exists already or does this seem useful?

Another idea: instead of a modifier, would it be possible to implement some quoting rules like shell? I've always felt ex commands were Vim's version of a shell.

```
:cmd arg  "not quoted, no escaping
:cmd 'arg'  "single quote, pass literally
```

I haven't thought much about this, so have no idea if this would work.
i'm exploring this idea

> instead of a modifier, would it be possible to implement some quoting rules like shell? I've always felt ex commands were Vim's version of a shell.

We would still need some way to opt-in to that because every command has its own rules.

> every command has its own rules.

Then a general solution might not be the best approach.

What's motivating this? I feel this sort of legacy should be kept frozen rather than stacking band aids on what is a bad design.

Would it not be easier and lower risk to just add API functions for the two cases you mentioned? I know you absolutely hate added surface area, but I think this is probably better than (yet another) modifier which needs to be specially implemented for every command individually.

There are 2 use-cases mentioned. This actually would avoid a lot of terrible bandaids which only exist to "escape" command args.



> modifier which needs to be specially implemented for every command individually.

That should not be necessary, but if it is then I agree it would be the wrong direction.

i agree 
> This actually would avoid a lot of terrible bandaids which only exist to "escape" command args.

we already rely on helpers like `fnameescape()` and `shellescape()`, and sometimes additional custom escaping logic `ses_escape_fname()`.these exist because of special parsing rules in cmd line. a mechanism like `:nospecial` could reduce need for such workarounds.

All use cases @justinmk mentioned seem to be for programming use, not interactive use. For that I think `nvim_cmd()` has already saved users from escaping space, quote, bar in command arguments.

For example,
```lua
vim.api.nvim_cmd({cmd = "edit", args = {'abc | "'}}, {})`
```
opens the file with name `abc | "` literally

While `:edit abc | "` open file named `abc`

~However, unfortunately, `nvim_cmd()` still requires users to escape `%`, `#` manually. Given that `nvim_cmd()` is already very close to your goal, would it make more sense to enhance it instead? For example, I think it `nvim_cmd()` should not expand `%`, `#` in args in the first place, I think people who use those shortcuts will unlikely use "verbose" API like `nvim_cmd()`~ See below comment

I think `nvim_cmd` can be passed ` magic = { file = false }` which avoids the need for some escaping.

>  magic: (dict) Which characters have special meaning in the command arguments.
> file: (boolean) The command expands filenames. Which means characters such as "%", "#" and wildcards are expanded.
bar: (boolean) The "|" character is treated as a command separator and the double quote character (") is treated as the start of a comment.

You're right. Off-topic here, but it is surprising to me that `magic.file` is `true` by default (I remember reading someone saying that an boolean option with no documented default is `false` by default)
That was indeed the intention of `nvim_cmd{magic}` but I thought it wasn't fully implemented. Part of the intention of this issue was to build something that can be used by `nvim_cmd`.

> That was indeed the intention of nvim_cmd{magic} but I thought it wasn't fully implemented

I have tested it, magic.file = false works correctly (no `%`, `#` expansion)

Example that doesn't work (but points to a `nvim_cmd` bug, rather than the need for `:nospecial`):

```
:lua vim.cmd.help({ '="%foo|bar#baz"', magic = { file = false, bar = false } })
E5108: Lua: [string ":lua"]:1: Vim:E149: Sorry, no help for ="%foo
```

I forgot another use-case (updated main description), which is interactive usage. For example:

    :nospecial edit https://example.com/#foo=bar%baz
    :nospecial buffer [test]

It's really annoying to have to escape `%`, `#`, `"`, `|` in filenames, shell commands (`:!`), etc.

- https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/31494

> ```
> :lua vim.cmd.help({ '="%foo|bar#baz"', magic = { file = false, bar = false } })
> E5108: Lua: [string ":lua"]:1: Vim:E149: Sorry, no help for ="%foo
> ```
it not working because `ex_help()`  has its own  `|` scanner. 
https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/765c063f0208b3b28958ba2f6e61669cecaa6045/src/nvim/help.c#L72-L76
and no access to `magic` tried to read `magic.bar` form `CmdParseInfo`  but it  dropped at `execute_cmd0()` 

notice if we call direct command,  args = null so added null check, but it is wrong it doesnot respect `magic`  we need to make `CmdParseInfo` accessible inside `ex_help()`.

<details>

```diff

diff --git a/src/nvim/help.c b/src/nvim/help.c
index 4babd8e4fc1d..c24e11e70b60 100644
--- a/src/nvim/help.c
+++ b/src/nvim/help.c
@@ -70,7 +70,7 @@ void ex_help(exarg_T *eap)
     // followed by some text.  Set nextcmd to the following command.
     for (arg = eap->arg; *arg; arg++) {
       if (*arg == '\n' || *arg == '\r'
-          || (*arg == '|' && arg[1] != NUL && arg[1] != '|')) {
+          || (eap->args == NULL && *arg == '|' && arg[1] != NUL && arg[1] != '|')) {
         *arg++ = NUL;
         eap->nextcmd = arg;
         break;
```
</details>
promoting the `magic` specification to the `exarg` struct makes a lot of sense I think.
i'll make a pr for refactor magic

labels.description: Need comments/opinions from people familiar with this topic.
labels.name: needs:discussion

milestone.description: We don't plan to work on this, but will accept high quality contributions from someone who will own the feature and follow up on bug reports.
milestone.title: needs-owner

title: `:nospecial cmd [args...]` invokes cmd with literal args

type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality
type.name: Enhancement
